hints: An internal force acting perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of a structural member
Causes one part of a beam to slide relative to an adjacent part
Visualised in a shear force diagram alongside the bending moment diagram
Maximum value usually occurs at supports or point loads
Paired with bending moment to fully describe the internal state of a loaded beam
Steel bars embedded in concrete to give it tensile strength
Concrete is strong in compression but weak in tension — this fixes the weakness
The ridged surface (deformations) helps it bond to the surrounding concrete
Corrosion of this element is the leading cause of concrete structure deterioration
Short for reinforcing bar; the combination of it and concrete is called reinforced concrete (RC)
The structural element that transfers loads from a building into the ground
Its design depends on soil bearing capacity and the loads above
Two broad categories: shallow (spread footings, mats) and deep (piles, caissons)
Getting it wrong is catastrophic — it's one of the hardest things to fix after construction
The Leaning Tower of Pisa's tilt is a famous example of inadequate soil investigation before construction
The internal tendency of a structural element to bend under load
The companion to shear force in beam analysis
Causes tension on one face of a beam and compression on the other
Maximum value in a simply supported beam with a central point load is at midspan
Formula: M = F × d, where F is force and d is the perpendicular distance
A structural framework made of triangular units
The triangle is used because it's the only shape that can't be deformed without changing member lengths
Members carry only axial forces (tension or compression) — no bending
Used in bridges, roof structures, and tower cranes
Common configurations include Pratt, Warren, and Howe trusses
The most widely used construction material in the world
Made from cement, water, sand, and aggregate (gravel or crushed rock)
Strong in compression, weak in tension — often reinforced with steel to compensate
Sets through a chemical hydration reaction, not by drying
Water-to-cement ratio is the single most important factor controlling its strength
The maximum load per unit area that a soil can support before failure
A key parameter in foundation design
Depends on soil type, density, water content, and depth of the foundation
Exceeding it causes settlement or sudden shear failure — the ground collapses under the structure
Determined by in-situ tests (SPT, CPT) or laboratory testing of soil samples
A structure that holds back soil or other material against a height difference
Found wherever roads are cut into hillsides, in basements, and along waterways
Must resist lateral earth pressure, hydrostatic pressure, and sometimes surcharge loads
Types include gravity, cantilever, sheet pile, and anchored walls
Drainage behind it is critical — water pressure is often more dangerous than soil pressure
The downward movement of a structure as the ground compresses under load
Some is expected — it becomes a problem when it's uneven or excessive
Differential settlement is more damaging than uniform settlement — it tilts and cracks structures
Can occur immediately (elastic) or over years (consolidation in clay soils)
The Millennium Tower in San Francisco is a well-known modern example of excessive post-construction settlement
A wall that carries loads from floors and roofs above down to the foundation
Removing one without proper support can cause partial or total collapse
Distinguished from partition walls, which only divide space and carry no structural load
Common in masonry and older construction; modern steel and concrete frames rely on columns instead
Engineers identify them by checking whether they sit on beams or foundations and carry floor joists above
The process of mechanically densifying soil to improve its strength and reduce settlement
Done with rollers, plate compactors, or rammers on site
Moisture content matters — too dry or too wet and the soil won't compact efficiently
Measured by comparing field density to the maximum from a Proctor test
Essential under roads, embankments, and building pads before construction begins
A deep foundation system that transfers loads to stronger soil or rock far below the surface
Used when surface soils are too weak to support a structure directly
Can be driven (hammered) or bored (drilled) into the ground
Load transfer happens through end bearing at the tip and skin friction along the shaft
The Marina Bay Sands hotel in Singapore sits on thousands of them
Concrete elements cast and cured in a factory before being transported to site
Higher quality control than in-situ concrete — factory conditions are more consistent
Speeds up construction — elements arrive ready to install
Used for beams, columns, floor slabs, wall panels, and bridge girders
Contrast with in-situ (cast-in-place) concrete, which is poured directly into formwork on site
The ratio of a structure's capacity to the actual load it experiences
A value of 1.0 means the structure is exactly at its limit — no margin
Building codes mandate minimum values to account for uncertainty and variability
Typical values range from 1.5 to 3.0 depending on consequences of failure and load certainty
Formula: FoS = Capacity / Demand — the higher it is, the safer the design
The science of water movement, distribution, and management on Earth
Civil engineers use it to design stormwater systems, dams, and flood protection
Involves estimating peak flows from rainfall events using catchment characteristics
The return period (e.g. 1-in-100-year flood) is a core concept
Underpins the design of culverts, retention ponds, and river channels
The displacement of a structural member from its original position under load
Too much of it causes cracking, discomfort, and damage to non-structural elements
Codes specify maximum allowable values — typically span/250 or span/360
Stiffer sections (larger I) and shorter spans reduce it
Calculated using integration of the bending moment diagram or standard formulas
The process of shaping the ground surface to a design elevation
Creates the correct slopes for drainage and prepares a site for construction
Involves cutting (removing soil) and filling (adding soil) to reach target levels
A grading plan shows contour lines of existing and finished ground
Balancing cut and fill minimises the cost of hauling material on or off site
A horizontal structural member that carries transverse loads
The most fundamental element in structural engineering
Resists load through bending and shear
Cross-section shape matters — I-beams are efficient because material is concentrated away from the neutral axis
Types include simply supported, cantilever, continuous, and fixed-fixed beams
A soil's ability to allow water to pass through it
Gravel has high permeability; clay has very low permeability
Critical for designing drainage systems, dams, and contaminant containment
Measured by Darcy's Law: Q = kiA, where k is the hydraulic conductivity
A low-permeability clay core in an earth dam prevents water from seeping through
Concrete in which internal stresses are deliberately introduced before loading
High-tensile steel tendons are tensioned to put the concrete into compression
Because concrete is strong in compression, it can then resist tension loads without cracking
Used in long-span bridges, parking structures, and floor slabs
Two methods: pre-tensioning (steel tensioned before concrete is poured) and post-tensioning (after)
The branch of civil engineering dealing with soil and rock behaviour
Informs foundation design, slope stability, and underground construction
Karl Terzaghi is considered the father of modern soil mechanics
Key tests include Standard Penetration Test (SPT) and Cone Penetration Test (CPT)
Uncertainty in soil properties makes it one of the most judgement-intensive disciplines in engineering
The application of materials or systems to prevent water ingress into a structure
Basements, tunnels, roofs, and bathrooms all require it
Failure leads to dampness, corrosion of reinforcement, and structural deterioration
Methods include membranes, crystalline admixtures, and drainage boards
The distinction between waterproofing (zero water) and damp-proofing (reduced moisture) matters in design
Steel sections used as the primary load-carrying elements in buildings and bridges
High strength, ductile, and fully recyclable
Standard sections include I-beams (wide flange), channels, angles, and hollow sections
Connections are made by bolting or welding
Steel frames allow open-plan layouts and fast construction — no need to wait for concrete to cure
The system for collecting and conveying surface and subsurface water
Prevents flooding, erosion, and waterlogging of structures and roads
Includes gutters, pipes, culverts, channels, and retention basins
Designed for a specific storm event (e.g. 10-year or 100-year return period)
Poor drainage is one of the most common causes of pavement and slope failures
A curved structural form that carries loads primarily in compression
Romans used them extensively — some 2000-year-old examples still stand
The curved geometry redirects vertical loads into diagonal thrusts at the supports
Extremely efficient in masonry and concrete, which are strong in compression
The Sydney Harbour Bridge and St. Louis Gateway Arch are iconic modern examples
Temporary molds into which concrete is poured and allowed to set
Must be strong enough to hold the liquid concrete without deflecting
Made from timber, steel, aluminium, or plastic panels
Accounts for a significant portion of in-situ concrete construction cost
Stripped after the concrete reaches sufficient strength; reusable systems reduce cost
The analysis of whether a slope will remain in place or fail
A slope fails when shear stresses exceed soil shear strength along a failure surface
Rainfall is the most common trigger — water raises pore pressure and reduces effective strength
Analysed using methods like Bishop's simplified method or limit equilibrium
Countermeasures include drainage, soil nailing, retaining walls, and reducing slope angle
The branch of civil engineering dealing with the safe and efficient movement of people and goods
Designs road geometries, signal timings, and intersection layouts
Uses traffic counts and flow models to predict congestion
Level of Service (LOS) A–F is the standard framework for rating road performance
Roundabouts, grade separations, and dynamic signal control are common tools
The science of measuring and mapping the Earth's surface
Essential before any construction project — you need to know exactly where things are
Instruments include total stations, GPS receivers, and laser levels
Produces topographic maps, boundary plans, and as-built records
Modern techniques include LiDAR scanning and drone photogrammetry for large-area surveys
The layered structure that forms the surface of a road, runway, or car park
Distributes vehicle loads so stresses reaching the subgrade are within acceptable limits
Two main types: flexible (asphalt over granular base) and rigid (concrete slab)
Design life is typically 20–40 years; distress modes include rutting, cracking, and potholes
Structural design uses CBR (California Bearing Ratio) of subgrade soil as a key input
